# TODOs
No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>

You are a repo maintainer enforcing a commit metadata policy. You have a repository at `/home/user/repo` which is already initialized with `jj`. Your task is to:
1. Configure `jj` locally in this repository to use a custom template for `jj log` by default. The template should output only the commit's short ID, the author's email, and the first line of the description, separated by ` | ` (e.g., `abc1234 | user@example.com | Fix bug`).
2. Create a new commit with the description "Update metadata policy" and ensure it is the current working copy.
3. Run `jj log -r @` and save the output to `/home/user/log_output.txt`.
I'll break this down into steps and execute them systematically.

todos.content: Configure jj locally to use custom log template (short ID | email | first line of description)
Create a new commit with description 'Update metadata policy'
Run jj log -r @ and save output to /home/user/log_output.txt

parts.text:
Now I have all the info I need. Let me configure the custom log template locally in the repository, create the commit, and run the log command.

**Step 1:** Configure the custom `jj log` template locally in the repo using `jj config set --repo`.

input.command: cd /home/user/repo && jj config set --repo templates.log 'short_id(commit_id) ++ " | " ++ author.email() ++ " | " ++ description.first_line() ++ "\n"'

parts.text: Let me check the exact syntax for jj config set:

output.output:
Update a config file to set the given option to a given value

Usage: jj config set [OPTIONS] <--user|--repo|--workspace> <NAME> <VALUE>

Arguments:
  <NAME>
          

  <VALUE>
          New value to set
          
          The value should be specified as a TOML expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any
          TOML constructs (such as apostrophes or array notation), quotes can be omitted. Note that
          the value may also need shell quoting. TOML multi-line strings can be useful if the value
          contains apostrophes. For example, to set `foo.bar` to the string "{don't}" use `jj config
          set --user foo.bar "'''{don't}'''"`. This is valid in both Bash and Fish.
          
          Alternative, e.g. to avoid dealing with shell quoting, use `jj config edit` to edit the
          TOML file directly.

Options:
      --user
          Target the user-level config

      --repo
          Target the repo-level config

      --workspace
          Target the workspace-level config

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Global Options:
  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>
          Path to repository to operate on
          
          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current
          working directory.

      --ignore-working-copy
          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it
          
          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The
          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the
          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead
          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be
          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the
          working copy.
          
          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies
          `--ignore-working-copy`.

      --ignore-immutable
          Allow rewriting immutable commits
          
          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.
          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.
          
          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or
          the `immutable` template keyword.

      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>
          Operation to load the repo at
          
          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent
          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.
          
          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier
          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would
          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the
          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.
          
          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation
          ID is enough.
          
          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if
          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.
          
          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.
          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier
          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.
          
          [aliases: --at-op]

      --debug
          Enable debug logging

      --color <WHEN>
          When to colorize output
          
          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]

      --quiet
          Silence non-primary command output
          
          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working
          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.
          
          Warnings and errors will still be printed.

      --no-pager
          Disable the pager

      --config <NAME=VALUE>
          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)
          
          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML
          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array
          notation), quotes can be omitted.

      --config-file <PATH>
          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)

parts.text: The value needs to be a TOML expression. Let me write the config file directly instead:
The file doesn't exist yet. Let me try using `jj config set` with proper TOML string quoting:

input.command: cd /home/user/repo && jj config set --repo templates.log "\"\"\"short_id(commit_id) ++ \" | \" ++ author.email() ++ \" | \" ++ description.first_line() ++ \"\\n\"\"\"\"" 2>&1

parts.text: Let me verify what got written:
There's an extra quote at the end. Let me check by testing it:
The template has a syntax issue. Let me fix the config file directly:
The TOML multi-line string has an issue. Let me write the config file directly with proper TOML quoting. In TOML, I'll use single-quoted strings (literal strings) to avoid escaping issues:
Let me see the actual error:

output.output:
Error: Failed to parse template: Function `short_id` doesn't exist
Caused by:  --> 1:1
  |
1 | short_id(commit_id) ++ " | " ++ author.email() ++ " | " ++ description.first_line() ++ "\n"
  | ^------^
  |
  = Function `short_id` doesn't exist
EXIT: 1

parts.text: The function is `short` not `short_id` in jj's template language. Let me check the correct syntax:

input.command: cd /home/user/repo && jj log --color never -T 'commit_id.short() ++ " | " ++ author.email() ++ " | " ++ description.first_line() ++ "\n"' 2>&1

parts.text: `commit_id.short()` is the correct syntax. But we want just the short ID without the graph prefix. However, looking at the expected format `abc1234 | user@example.com | Fix bug`, the template controls only the content, not the graph. Let me also check if there's a way to just get the short change ID. The task says "short ID" which likely means the short commit ID. Let me update the config:
The custom template is working. Now let me mark step 1 as complete and move on to creating the commit.

todos.content: Configure jj locally to use custom log template (short ID | email | first line of description)
Create a new commit with description 'Update metadata policy'
Run jj log -r @ and save output to /home/user/log_output.txt

parts.text: **Step 2:** Create a new commit with the description "Update metadata policy" and ensure it is the current working copy.

Parent commit (@-)      : tmtllnzo 3d087b03 (empty) Initial commit

○  3d087b031c19 | test@example.com | Initial commit
○  5e3186bbc43e | test@example.com |
◆  000000000000 |  |

parts.text: The commit "Update metadata policy" is the current working copy (`@`). Now step 3:

Create a new commit with description 'Update metadata policy'
Run jj log -r @ and save output to /home/user/log_output.txt

parts.text: **Step 3:** Run `jj log -r @` and save the output to `/home/user/log_output.txt`.
The output file contains the expected format with the custom template applied. Let me mark all tasks complete:

todos.content: Configure jj locally to use custom log template (short ID | email | first line of description)
Create a new commit with description 'Update metadata policy'
Run jj log -r @ and save output to /home/user/log_output.txt

input.result:
All three tasks have been completed successfully:

1. **Custom log template configured locally** — The repo-level jj config at `/root/.config/jj/repos/89a80288c6e06f5094fb/config.toml` now sets `templates.log` to:
   ```
   commit_id.short() ++ " | " ++ author.email() ++ " | " ++ description.first_line() ++ "\n"
   ```
   This outputs each commit as `<short_id> | <email> | <first_line_of_description>`.

2. **New commit created** — A commit with the description "Update metadata policy" was created using `jj new -m "Update metadata policy"` and is the current working copy (`@`).

3. **Log output saved** — `jj log -r @` was run and the output was saved to `/home/user/log_output.txt`. Contents:
   ```
   @  589f2e5005ac | test@example.com | Update metadata policy
   ```
